items.myth: k-NN has no hyperparameters because it does not train.
items.reality: k is the primary hyperparameter; distance metric, weighting scheme, and preprocessing (scaling, feature subset) also require choices and validation tuning.
items.example: Same data with k = 1 vs k = 15 can differ by double-digit validation accuracy.

items.reality: Euclidean (L₂) is the common default, but Manhattan, Minkowski, cosine, and domain-specific metrics are valid — see Distance Metrics and Norms and Distances.
items.example: Text embeddings often use cosine similarity; k-NN with metric='cosine' in sklearn.

items.myth: More training data always makes k-NN faster and better.
items.reality: More data can improve accuracy but increases memory and query cost linearly for naive search; high d can make extra data help little due to distance concentration.
items.example: Doubling n doubles per-query scan time unless an index structure is rebuilt.

items.myth: k-NN and k-means are the same algorithm because both use k.
items.reality: k-NN is supervised instance-based prediction from labeled neighbors; k-means is unsupervised clustering that partitions unlabeled points into k centroids — different k, different objective.
items.example: k-NN predicts label from neighbor votes; k-means assigns cluster IDs without labels.
